text:
The sock disappearance problem is one of science's great unsolved mysteries, and I have done the research.

Socks are sentient. Not in a threatening way, just enough to be inconvenient. When you load the washing machine, each sock performs a rapid calculation: am I paired with someone I like? If the answer is no, it phases out of existence. The washing machine is not losing socks. It is facilitating breakups.

The dryer is different. The dryer is a portal. Not a big dramatic interdimensional one, just a small bureaucratic one that opens briefly at around 140 degrees Fahrenheit and accepts exactly one sock per load as a kind of toll. Where do the socks go? A parallel dimension that is entirely socks. It is very soft there, and nothing matches.

Some people blame static electricity. Static electricity is not responsible, but it appreciates being mentioned because it rarely gets credit for anything.

The real culprit, confirmed by my own extensive observation, is the space between the dryer drum and the dryer door seal. Socks compress, slip through, and accumulate in a hidden pocket of the machine until you move apartments and someone pulls the dryer away from the wall and finds seventeen socks and a 2019 receipt from a gas station.

The solution is to buy identical socks, all the same color and brand, so that any survivor can pair with any other survivor. This works. Corporations hate it. The sock industry runs on the grief of mismatched pairs.

You can also pin your socks together before washing them. This works too but you will feel slightly defeated by laundry, which is its own kind of loss.

Anyway the socks are gone. They are probably happier.
